title: Enums for ADTs
oldCode:
sealed trait Color
case object Red extends Color
case object Green extends Color
case object Blue extends Color
modernCode:
enum Color:
  case Red, Green, Blue
summary: Replace sealed trait hierarchies with clean enum syntax
explanation: Scala 3 enums provide a concise way to define ADTs. They compile to the same sealed hierarchy but with much less boilerplate.

title: Given instances (implicit replacement)
oldCode:
implicit val ordering: Ordering[Person] =
  Ordering.by(_.age)

def sort[A](list: List[A])
  (implicit ord: Ordering[A]): List[A] =
  list.sorted
modernCode:
given Ordering[Person] =
  Ordering.by(_.age)

def sort[A](list: List[A])
  (using Ordering[A]): List[A] =
  list.sorted
summary: Replace implicit with given/using for contextual abstractions
explanation: Given instances make implicit parameters explicit and easier to understand. The 'using' keyword clarifies parameter passing.

title: Extension methods
modernCode:
extension (s: String)
  def exclaim: String = s + "!"
  def question: String = s + "?"

"hello".exclaim
summary: Define extension methods with cleaner syntax
explanation: Extension methods in Scala 3 replace implicit classes with a more direct syntax that clearly shows the intent.

title: Union types
summary: Use union types instead of sealed traits for simple cases
explanation: Union types provide a lightweight way to express 'either/or' types without creating a full ADT hierarchy.

title: Intersection types
modernCode:
trait Resetable:
  def reset(): Unit

trait Closeable:
  def close(): Unit

def cleanup(resource: Resetable & Closeable) =
  resource.reset()
  resource.close()
summary: Use & for intersection types with cleaner syntax
explanation: Intersection types use the & operator, making the intent clearer and syntax more consistent with union types.

title: Top-level definitions
modernCode:
package com.example

def greet(name: String): String =
  s"Hello, $name!"

val PI = 3.14159
summary: Define functions and values at top level without wrapping object
explanation: Scala 3 allows top-level definitions, eliminating the need for wrapper objects for utility functions and constants.

title: Optional braces (indentation syntax)
modernCode:
def fibonacci(n: Int): Int =
  if n <= 1 then
    n
  else
    fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2)
summary: Use significant indentation instead of braces
explanation: Scala 3 supports Python-style indentation syntax, making code cleaner and more readable while maintaining full compatibility with brace syntax.

title: Opaque type aliases
modernCode:
opaque type UserId = String

object UserId:
  def apply(s: String): UserId = s

  extension (id: UserId)
    def value: String = id

val id: UserId = UserId("user123")
summary: Use opaque types for zero-cost type safety
explanation: Opaque types provide type safety at compile time with zero runtime overhead, replacing value classes.

title: Match types
modernCode:
type Elem[X] = X match
  case String => Char
  case Array[t] => t
  case Iterable[t] => t
  case Int => String
summary: Pattern match on types at compile time
explanation: Match types allow type-level pattern matching, replacing complex type projections and implicit machinery.

title: Inline methods
modernCode:
inline def debug(msg: String): Unit =
  inline if Config.debugEnabled then
    println(s"[DEBUG] $msg")
summary: Use inline for zero-cost abstractions and compile-time evaluation
explanation: Inline methods are expanded at compile time, enabling dead code elimination and constant folding for better performance.

title: Enums with parameters
oldCode:
sealed trait HttpStatus
case class Ok(body: String) extends HttpStatus
case class NotFound(path: String) extends HttpStatus
case object ServerError extends HttpStatus
modernCode:
enum HttpStatus:
  case Ok(body: String)
  case NotFound(path: String)
  case ServerError
summary: Enums support parameters just like case classes
explanation: Scala 3 enums can have parameters, combining the conciseness of enums with the power of ADTs.

title: Export clauses
modernCode:
class Wrapper(val inner: Service):
  export inner.*
summary: Export members from another object without delegation boilerplate
explanation: Export clauses automatically forward members, eliminating manual delegation code.

title: Multiversal equality
summary: Type-safe equality prevents comparing incompatible types
explanation: Multiversal equality makes == type-safe, catching comparison errors at compile time.

title: Creator applications (no new)
oldCode:
class Person(name: String, age: Int)

val p = new Person("Alice", 30)
modernCode:
class Person(name: String, age: Int)

val p = Person("Alice", 30)
summary: Create instances without new keyword
explanation: Scala 3 allows omitting new for class instantiation, making syntax more uniform.

title: Context functions
modernCode:
type Transactional[T] = Connection ?=> T

def transaction[T](block: Transactional[T])
  (using Connection): T =
  block

transaction:
  summon[Connection].execute("INSERT...")
summary: Context functions abstract over using clauses
explanation: Context function types (?=>) allow you to abstract over contextual parameters elegantly.

title: Type lambdas
summary: Native type lambda syntax without compiler plugins
explanation: Scala 3 has built-in type lambda syntax using [X] =>> ..., no plugins needed.

title: Polymorphic function types
modernCode:
val reverser: [T] => List[T] => List[T] =
  [T] => (list: List[T]) => list.reverse

val result = reverser(List(1, 2, 3))
summary: First-class polymorphic functions without trait wrappers
explanation: Polymorphic function types allow expressing polymorphic functions as values directly.

title: Dependent function types
modernCode:
trait Entry:
  type Value
  def value: Value

val extractor: (e: Entry) => e.Value =
  (e: Entry) => e.value
summary: Functions can depend on value parameters at the type level
explanation: Dependent function types enable return types that depend on value parameters.

title: Explicit implicit conversions
oldCode:
implicit def stringToInt(s: String): Int =
  s.toInt

val x: Int = "42" // Automatic conversion
modernCode:
import scala.language.implicitConversions

given Conversion[String, Int] with
  def apply(s: String): Int = s.toInt

val x: Int = "42" // Explicit Conversion
summary: Implicit conversions require explicit Conversion type
explanation: Scala 3 makes implicit conversions safer by requiring explicit Conversion type.

title: Improved structural types
summary: Structural types work without reflection imports
explanation: Scala 3 structural types are more efficient and don't require language imports.

title: LazyList replaces Stream
oldCode:
val naturals = Stream.from(1)
val evens = naturals.filter(_ % 2 == 0)
evens.take(5).toList
modernCode:
val naturals = LazyList.from(1)
val evens = naturals.filter(_ % 2 == 0)
evens.take(5).toList
summary: LazyList replaces deprecated Stream for lazy sequences
explanation: Stream was deprecated in 2.13. LazyList has better memory behavior and clearer semantics.

title: JSON with circe (modern approach)
modernCode:
import io.circe.*, io.circe.generic.auto.*
import io.circe.parser.*

case class User(name: String, age: Int)

val user = decode[User](jsonString)
summary: Circe provides automatic JSON codec derivation
explanation: Circe uses compile-time derivation for type-safe JSON handling without reflection.

title: XML literals (still available)
oldCode:
val xml = <user><name>Alice</name></user>
val name = (xml \\ "name").text
summary: XML literals still exist but libraries are preferred
explanation: XML literals work in Scala 3, but typed libraries like scalaxb are now preferred.

title: ArraySeq for immutable arrays
oldCode:
val arr = Array(1, 2, 3).toSeq
// Returns mutable WrappedArray
modernCode:
val arr = ArraySeq(1, 2, 3)
// Truly immutable with array performance
summary: ArraySeq provides immutable array-backed sequences
explanation: ArraySeq.unsafeWrapArray gives O(1) operations with immutable guarantees.

title: Improved collection views
oldCode:
val nums = List(1, 2, 3)
nums.view.map(_ * 2).force
modernCode:
val nums = List(1, 2, 3)
nums.view.map(_ * 2).to(List)
summary: Views use .to() instead of .force
explanation: Scala 2.13 redesigned views with better type safety and performance.

title: Future.transform simplification
oldCode:
future.transform(
  success => Success(transform(success)),
  failure => Failure(failure)
)
summary: transform now accepts a single Try => Try function
explanation: Unified transform API is simpler and more composable.

title: Using for resource management
modernCode:
import scala.util.Using

Using(Source.fromFile("file.txt")) { source =>
  source.getLines().toList
}
summary: Using provides automatic resource management
explanation: Using handles resource cleanup safely, returning Try[T] with proper error handling.

title: groupMap and groupMapReduce
oldCode:
val words = List("a", "bb", "ccc")
words.groupBy(_.length)
  .mapValues(_.map(_.toUpperCase))
modernCode:
val words = List("a", "bb", "ccc")
words.groupMap(_.length)(_.toUpperCase)
summary: groupMap combines groupBy and map in one operation
explanation: More efficient than groupBy + mapValues, avoids creating intermediate collections.

title: tapEach for side effects
oldCode:
val list = List(1, 2, 3)
list.foreach(println)
list.map(_ * 2)
modernCode:
val list = List(1, 2, 3)
list.tapEach(println).map(_ * 2)
summary: tapEach chains side effects without breaking the flow
explanation: tapEach returns the collection, enabling fluent side-effectful operations.

title: Pipe and tap for chaining
modernCode:
import scala.util.chaining.*

val result = value
  .pipe(function1)
  .pipe(function2)
  .pipe(function3)
summary: Pipe operator enables forward chaining of operations
explanation: The pipe method (|>) makes data transformation pipelines more readable.

title: Automatic typeclass derivation
modernCode:
trait Show[A]:
  def show(a: A): String

object Show:
  given Show[Int] with
    def show(a: Int) = a.toString

  // Or derive automatically:
  inline given derived[T]: Show[T] =
    compiletime.summonInline[Mirror.Of[T]]
summary: Scala 3 enables compile-time typeclass derivation
explanation: Using inline and Mirror, typeclasses can be derived automatically without macros.

title: Typeclass instance resolution
oldCode:
def serialize[A](a: A)
  (implicit s: Serializer[A]): String =
  s.serialize(a)

serialize(data)
summary: summon replaces implicitly for clearer intent
explanation: summon[T] is the preferred way to summon typeclass instances in Scala 3.

title: Typeclass syntax with extensions
summary: Extensions integrate naturally with typeclasses
explanation: Extension methods with using clauses provide clean typeclass syntax.

title: Cleaner for comprehensions
modernCode:
for
  x <- xs
  y <- ys
  if x > 0
  z <- zs
yield (x, y, z)
summary: For comprehensions work with indentation syntax
explanation: Scala 3 optional braces make for comprehensions even cleaner.

title: Pattern bindings in for
modernCode:
for
  (x, y) <- pairs
  if x > 0
yield x + y
summary: Direct pattern matching in generators
explanation: Pattern matching in generators is more natural than separate bindings.

title: Try with pattern matching
modernCode:
import scala.util.{Try, Success, Failure}

Try(riskyOperation()) match
  case Success(result) =>
    println(s"Success: $result")
  case Failure(e: IOException) =>
    println(s"IO Error: ${e.getMessage}")
  case Failure(e) =>
    println(s"Error: ${e.getMessage}")
summary: Try wraps exceptions as Success/Failure values
explanation: Try makes error handling functional and composable with map, flatMap, etc.

title: Either for typed errors
modernCode:
enum MathError:
  case DivisionByZero

def divide(a: Int, b: Int): Either[MathError, Int] =
  if b == 0 then
    Left(MathError.DivisionByZero)
  else
    Right(a / b)
summary: Either provides type-safe error handling without exceptions
explanation: Either[Error, Success] makes errors explicit in function signatures.

title: Option instead of null
summary: Option eliminates null pointer exceptions
explanation: Option[T] explicitly represents presence/absence, preventing null errors.

title: Flatten nested Options/Eithers
modernCode:
val result = for
  user <- getUser(id)
  addr <- getAddress(user)
yield addr.city
summary: For comprehensions flatten nested monadic operations
explanation: For comprehensions make chaining Option/Either/Try operations elegant.

title: Validated for error accumulation (Cats)
oldCode:
// Either stops at first error
for
  age <- validateAge(data.age)
  email <- validateEmail(data.email)
  name <- validateName(data.name)
yield User(name, age, email)
modernCode:
import cats.data.Validated
import cats.implicits.*

(validateAge(data.age),
 validateEmail(data.email),
 validateName(data.name)
).mapN(User.apply)
// Returns all validation errors
summary: Validated accumulates all errors instead of failing fast
explanation: Validated is better for form validation where you want to show all errors.

title: Boundary/break for early returns
summary: Boundary provides controlled non-local returns
explanation: Boundary/break offers structured early returns without exceptions or vars.

title: Using with multiple resources
modernCode:
import scala.util.Using

Using.Manager { use =>
  val in = use(new FileInputStream("in.txt"))
  val out = use(new FileOutputStream("out.txt"))
  // All resources closed automatically
  copyStream(in, out)
}
summary: Using.Manager handles multiple resources safely
explanation: Manager ensures all resources are closed even if some acquisitions fail.

title: Named tuples
oldCode:
val person = ("Alice", 30, "Engineer")
val name = person._1
val age = person._2
val job = person._3
modernCode:
val person = (name = "Alice", age = 30, job = "Engineer")
val name = person.name
val age = person.age
val job = person.job
summary: Named tuples provide field access by name instead of position
explanation: Named tuples are lightweight records without boilerplate, compile to regular tuples.

title: Named tuple types
modernCode:
type Person = (name: String, age: Int)

def greet(p: Person): String =
  s"Hello ${p.name}, age ${p.age}"

val p: Person = (name = "Alice", age = 30)
summary: Named tuple types replace simple case classes
explanation: For simple data holders, named tuples avoid case class overhead.

title: Capture checking (experimental)
summary: Capture checking prevents capturing escaping references
explanation: CC mode tracks which values are captured, preventing use-after-free bugs.

title: Explicit nulls (experimental)
oldCode:
val s: String = null // Compiles
s.length // NullPointerException
modernCode:
// With -Yexplicit-nulls:
val s: String = null // Error!
val s: String | Null = null // OK
s.length // Error: might be null
s.nn.length // OK: nn asserts non-null
summary: Explicit nulls make null safety compile-time enforced
explanation: String is non-nullable by default, use String | Null for nullable.

title: Fewer braces syntax
oldCode:
def compute =
  val x = 1
  val y = 2
  x + y
modernCode:
def compute =
  val x = 1
  val y = 2
  x + y

// Works with trailing colons:
List(1,2,3).map:
  x => x * 2
summary: Colon syntax enables trailing lambda without braces
explanation: Trailing colon allows skipping braces for single-arg lambdas.

title: Infix type operators
oldCode:
type Result[E, A] = Either[E, A]
val x: Result[String, Int] = Right(42)
modernCode:
// Natural infix notation:
val x: String Either Int = Right(42)
val y: String | Int = "hello"
val z: Readable & Writable = resource
summary: Type operators can be used infix naturally
explanation: Two-parameter types read more naturally with infix notation.

title: Quotes and splices (macros)
summary: Quotes and splices replace Scala 2 macro system
explanation: Scala 3 macros use quotes '{ } and splices ${ } for type-safe AST manipulation.

title: Transparent inline
oldCode:
inline def choose(cond: Boolean,
  thenp: => Int, elsep: => Int): Int =
  if cond then thenp else elsep

// Return type always Int
modernCode:
transparent inline def choose(cond: Boolean,
  thenp: => Any, elsep: => Any) =
  if cond then thenp else elsep

val x = choose(true, "hi", 42)
// x has type String, not Any!
summary: Transparent inline refines return type based on parameters
explanation: Transparent inline allows the return type to be computed from argument types.

title: Compiletime operations
oldCode:
def size[T](value: T): Int =
  // Computed at runtime
modernCode:
import scala.compiletime.*

inline def size[T <: Tuple]: Int =
  constValue[Tuple.Size[T]]

size[(Int, String, Boolean)] // 3, computed at compile time!
summary: Compiletime operations move computations to compile time
explanation: Operations on types and constants can be evaluated during compilation.

title: @main annotation for entry points
summary: @main generates entry point with automatic arg parsing
explanation: @main methods get automatic command-line parsing and help messages.

title: Thread safety annotations
summary: Annotations help track thread-unsafe mutable state
explanation: @threadUnsafe marks state that shouldn't be accessed concurrently.

title: Improved type inference
summary: Scala 3 has much better type inference
explanation: Better principal type inference reduces need for type annotations.
